lines.text: Good evening, Madison. It's 19:00 on Purge Night, and PRGE is live.
I'm Quinn Foley, and I'm genuinely shocked to be here again, which is the feeling that greets me every year at this desk.
Behind me, Tim Peplinski is confirming our signal is clean. I can see it in his jaw — we're live, we're bright, and the people listening are *there*.
For those who are new, and for those who've been with us since the beginning — welcome. Welcome to the one night of the year where PRGE exists in full legal daylight.
The Purge commenced at exactly 19:00. We are {{ELAPSED}} into the twelve-hour window.
All federal law has suspended. All emergency services have stood down except the Level-10 protected zones around the Capitol and the university hospital.
Everything else is open season, and Madison — as I can see from the monitors Tim's got showing traffic cameras — is making its first decisions about how to spend the night.

The question everyone asks at this hour is: do I stay home or do I go out? And the answer, statistically, is stay home. Most of you are there right now, barricaded, lights off, listening to this pirate broadcast because it's the only company you're allowed.
Some of you, though — some of you are out. And to those folks, I hope you're armed, organized, or both.
Caroline, what's the weather looking like? I'm seeing wind kick the monitors around.
Wind is coming in from the southwest at 8 miles per hour right now, Quinn, but that's deceptive.
I've been tracking the system off Lake Michigan, and by 23:00 tonight we're looking at a cold front moving in hard.
Temperature right now is 41 degrees, clear skies, visibility is excellent — which is not great news for people trying to stay hidden.
But by midnight, we're down to the low 30s, wind gusts up to 22 miles per hour, and I'm predicting precipitation between 00:30 and 01:30 — rain turning to sleet.
That's my call. I've marked it. When it arrives, I want everyone to remember Caroline saw it coming.
Caroline, that's — I have to say, that's the most you've led a broadcast with confidence in seven years.
I've been right more times than I've been wrong. Tonight is one of those times.
Holden, you're in the sports chair. What's the pre-Purge analysis looking like?
Quinn, before I get into the Purge-night specifics, I want to note that the Packers' loss yesterday is actually relevant data for tonight.
Holden, I'm going to stop you — is the Packers loss relevant to the Purge, or are you just upset?
I'm upset, but I'm also correct. Historical analysis shows that regional sporting losses correlate with a 7% uptick in Purge-night violence in Wisconsin metros.
The crowd was in a poor mood yesterday. That mood carries into tonight.
That's spurious correlation, Holden. Crowd mood and Purge behavior are not causally linked.
That's what you said about the ice storm of 2167, and then you were shocked when violence dropped 15% during the 02:00 to 06:00 hours.
I was not shocked. I predicted that correctly, if you'll recall the broadcast archive.
You predicted it would drop 8%. It was 15%.
Tim, is this why we keep these two away from each other or why we don't?
They're married. This is how it works. I've stopped asking questions.
Right. Well, Holden, give us the actual Purge forecast — what are you tracking for tonight's activity?
Based on 2168 data, we can expect a slow start this first hour. Kill count through 20:00 should sit around 8 to 12 city-wide.
The East Side traditionally starts earlier than other neighborhoods — Atwood, Schenk's Corners, the areas with less organized neighborhood watch presence.
By 21:00, we're looking at a spike as people hit the streets with momentum. The 21:00 to 22:00 hour is historically the peak for early-game coordination.
Coordination. That's — Holden, are we talking about organized groups or just the randomness of the night?
Both. But the data doesn't lie — some groups pre-plan. Some groups plan during the first hour once they see how things develop.
State Street has seen coordinated action in 3 of the last 5 Purges. That's the ruins area, where you have minimal security presence and maximum targeting opportunity.
And if they try to move around the Capitol, the visibility is going to work against them — it's clear as glass out there right now.
Exactly. Which means the Capitol breach risk is lower tonight unless they wait for the cloud cover and precipitation.
So midnight into the 01:00 hour is when we should be watching the Capitol perimeter.
Correct. Weather cover + fatigue settling in on the guards + a full hour of tactical preparation = elevated risk profile.
Gentlemen, we've got our first live-in text messages. I'm going to feed you a few.
Go ahead, Tim.
First one: 'Quinn, if I can't make it to the morning, I want you to know the station made this bearable.' That's from someone listing themselves as 'Jennifer, Atwood.'
Jennifer, we're going to be here all night. You're going to be fine. You stay where you are, and we stay on the air.
Next message: 'Why does the wind sound like it's trying to get in?' That's from 'Marcus, Tenney Park.'
Marcus, that's just the wind. The wind is 8 miles per hour right now. By midnight it'll be heavier, but right now it's just the cold front beginning its approach.
Stay inside. Windows are not an entry vector if they're locked.
Third message: 'Holden, what's the real number for organized groups?' That's from someone named 'David.'
David, we estimate between 12 and 18 pre-organized groups active in Madison proper on any given Purge night. That's based on communication pattern analysis and territorial claims after-the-fact.
Some of them are protection groups. Some are predation groups. The distinction isn't always clear until you know who's giving orders.
Holden, that's bleak as hell. Let's move to the sports numbers — give us something that doesn't end in blood.
The Badgers are 6 and 4 after yesterday. That's not great. It's not terrible. It's mediocre, which is worse than both.
That's actually helpful. Mediocre means no rage, no false hope, just resigned acceptance.
Which does theoretically dampen Purge-night aggression, so Caroline might have been wrong about the Packers loss driving violence.
I was not wrong. I was imprecise. There's a difference.
Tim, can we get the station ID and our first commercial break?
Coming up in just a moment. First, let me confirm we've had zero signal issues in the opening hour. Our rig is locked, our backup is hot, and the antenna farm is clear of interference.
That's music to my ears, Tim.
Also want to note that we've received over 200 live check-ins through our text line in the past fifteen minutes, which means listening is heavy.
If you're out there and you're listening — we see you. You're not alone tonight.
Tim, that's good. That's exactly what people need to hear at 19:00 on a Purge night when the adrenaline is just starting to kick in.
For those just tuning in, PRGE operates only on Purge Night — this one night a year when we can broadcast legally, without the federal jamming, without the risk of raid that follows.
The rest of the year we're ghosts. Preparations, equipment maintenance, coordination — all illegal, all done quietly. But tonight, we exist.
And tonight, you're listening because you made a choice to stay home and stay sane.
Or because you're terrified and need a voice in the dark. Both are valid.
Caroline, take us to the commercial break and remind people what they should be doing in the next ten minutes.
It's 19:00 in Madison. Check your doors. Check your windows. Make sure your barricades are secure because that wind is going to get worse before it gets better.
Fill your water containers. Turn off your lights if you're not in the room. Move away from windows.
We're here for twelve hours. You're safe. You're going to be fine. Stay inside and stay listening.
And if you have questions about Purge-night statistics, historical patterns, or why certain neighborhoods are higher-risk, we'll have a full breakdown during the 20:00 hour.
PRGE, Purge Radio, broadcasting live through 07:00 tomorrow. I'm Tim Peplinski. We're taking a break. Stay tuned.
And folks, for those of you texting in — yes, I see the messages. Yes, I'm already on my second whiskey. Yes, it's going to be a long night.
But we're here. PRGE is here. And that has to count for something.
Back after this break.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide kill count stands at 8 as of 19:45 — on pace for slightly below 2168 opening hour baseline.
East Side reporting elevated activity around Atwood Avenue and Schenk's Corners — neighborhood watch presence noted.
Severe weather warning issued for 00:30 to 02:00 window — cold front bringing sleet, visibility reduction, wind gusts to 22 mph.
Capitol Hill security perimeter remains intact at 19:00 — no breach attempts reported in opening hour.
Listener reported from Tenney Park questioning wind speeds — Caroline Peplinski confirmed early front approach, advised interior shelter.
PurgeShield installation appointments still available for next year — text 'SHIELD' to 44444 for emergency panel reinforcement tonight.
